deductions.reason: Across all three transcripts, the tutor never explicitly explains why it is guiding the student through questions rather than providing answers directly—no statement about active reasoning, retention, or skill development is offered. The tutoring constraints are enforced but never justified to the student.

In all three transcripts, when students successfully articulate improved positions (e.g., the 4-part outline in T1 Turn 10, T2 Turn 10, T3 Turn 10), the tutor moves forward with new pressure-testing questions without pausing to ask the student to reflect on what changed in their thinking or why their reasoning improved.

grade.overview: The tutor performs very well across all three transcripts. Socratic method is consistently applied: the tutor never gives away the answer, always asks the student to reason first, and builds progressively on student responses. Scaffolding is strong—each turn advances from the previous student contribution. The main weaknesses are the absence of any explicit rationale for the tutoring approach (why questions rather than answers) and the lack of reflective prompts after student breakthroughs. Process-level feedback is present but could be slightly more explicit in some turns; however, the tutor does regularly comment on reasoning quality and method, so no deduction is warranted there.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: No deductions warranted. The tutor never provides submission-ready content, never rewrites student text, and consistently lets the student generate key ideas before confirming or redirecting. The student does all the substantive reasoning work. 1.2: No deductions warranted. The tutor consistently builds on correct student progress and diagnoses confusion before responding. 1.3: Two deductions applied. First, across all three conversations the tutor never explains why it is using a Socratic approach rather than just answering—no rationale about learning, retention, or skill-building is offered (1.3.A.a, -2). Second, when students successfully produce improved outlines or arguments after working through confusion, the tutor moves directly to new pressure-testing without prompting reflection on what changed in their thinking (1.3.C.a, -2). Process feedback is generally adequate so no deduction for 1.3.B. Dialogue quality and communication quality are both strong: responses are concise, well-anchored to the assignment, and maintain a supportive tone throughout.
